macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rav and Shmuel: Does Majority Constitute Proof in Monetary Disputes (Holchin B'Mamon Achar Ha'Rov)?

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Core Debate: A Goring Ox as Mekach Taus
Generalizing the Debate: Holchin B'Mamon Achar Ha'Rov and the Burden of Proof
Rav's Position: Majority Constitutes Proof Against Chazaka
Shmuel's Position: Majority Does Not Overcome Chazaka in Monetary Disputes

macro_segments.title: The Slave with Defects: Attempting to Prove the Debate Through Talmudic Precedent

micro_segments.title: The Case of a Slave Found to Be a Burglar or Gambler (Kubyustos)
Buyer Beware Principle: Minor Defects vs. Severe Conditions (Death Sentence)
Gemara's Challenge: Is the Slave Case Really About Majority or About Something Else?
The Gemara's Rejection: All Slaves Are Like That, Not Just the Majority

macro_segments.title: The Pregnant Cow Gored and the Question of Miscarriage: Rov vs. Safek in Causation

micro_segments.title: The Case of an Ox Goring a Pregnant Cow: When Did the Miscarriage Occur?
The Sumchus Position: Mamona Hamutal B'Safek Cholkin (Splitting the Difference)
Can Rov Determine What Happened? The Gemara's Challenge
Distinguishing Between Goring and Fear: The Safek Within the Rov
Tosafot's Framing: Multiple Possibilities Create a Safek Despite Rov
Conclusion: The Case Does Not Prove the Debate Because a True Rov Is Lacking

macro_segments.title: The Debated Tannaim: Ox with Propensity to Gore (Chazaka) vs. Camel in Heat

micro_segments.title: An Ox Known to Gore Found Near a Dead Ox: Circumstantial Evidence
Rabbi Acha's Position: Even Without Direct Knowledge, Use Circumstantial Evidence
Hypothetical Connection to Rav and Shmuel's Debate on Rov and Chazaka
Rav's Clarification: Rov Can Be Used Without Chazaka, and Vice Versa
Shmuel's Clarification: Chazaka (Propensity) Is Weightier Than General Rov (Statistics)
The Conceptual Difference: Propensity About Specific Individuals vs. General Statistics

macro_segments.title: Flaxseed as a Test Case: Does Majority Usage Constitute Mekach Taus?

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Even Flaxseed (Which Most Buy for Planting) Does Not Constitute Mekach Taus
The Baraita Positions: Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Yossi on When to Apply Mekach Taus
The Amru Lo Objection: Many People Buy Flaxseed for Other Purposes
Gemara's Reframing: Is It the Majority of People or the Majority of Seeds?
Conclusion: The Mishnah Represents Shmuel's Position; Rabbi Yossi and Amru Lo Represent Rav

macro_segments.title: Liability for Defective Products: From Seeds to Catering and Professional Negligence

micro_segments.title: Reimbursement for Mekach Taus: Seed Cost Only or Including Expenses?
The Tanna Kamma vs. Yesh Omrim: Seed Cost Only vs. Including Farmer's Expenses
Rav Chisda's Attribution: Yesh Omrim Is the Position of Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel
The Baraita of the Miller, Baker, and Butcher: Professional Negligence and Liability
The Catering Disaster: Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel's Broader Liability for Embarrassment
The Tablecloth Custom: A Beautiful Jerusalem Practice for Welcoming Guests
Conceptual Basis: Professional Liability and Foreseeability of Consequential Damages

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Shomer Liability: Negligence Standard and the Nature of Adam Hamazik

micro_segments.title: Why Is Liability for Craftspeople Tied to Payment Status?
Tosafot's Position: Adam Hamazik Requires Some Negligence; Complete Ones Is Exempt
Ramban's Position: Liability Depends on the Relationship to the Object (Uman vs. Mazik)
The Ramban's Alternative Approach: Responsibility of the Damaged Party (Peshias Hanizak)

macro_segments.title: Standard Allowances for Defective Goods: Dirt, Worms, and Mixed Legumes in Produce

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Mishnah's List of Acceptable Defect Rates in Agricultural Products
Wheat and Chaff: A Quarter Kav of Extraneous Material Per Se'ah (About 4 Percent)
Figs: Up to 10 Percent Wormy (Mesulaos)
Wine Cellar: Up to 10 Percent Spoiled Barrels
Jugs in Sharon Valley: Up to 10 Percent Imperfect (Pitzetzos)
Rav Katina's Teaching: Legumes and Wild Plants vs. Pure Dirt—Different Standards
The Distinction: Legumes and Mixed Vegetation Are Anticipated; Dirt Is Not
